title: Dynamic Programming
description: Finally resolving Fibonacci's O(2ⁿ) cliffhanger from Recursion in DSA: the two properties that make DP applicable, and a preview of its two approaches.
objectives: Know the two required properties: overlapping subproblems and optimal substructure
Connect DP directly back to naive Fibonacci's inefficiency
Preview memoization and tabulation as the two DP approaches

sections.text: 1. Recap: The Cliffhanger from Recursion in DSA

Recursion in DSA ended with a promise: naive Fibonacci's O(2ⁿ) time, caused by overlapping subproblems (fibonacci(2) computed repeatedly, independently, many times), would be fixed by "remembering an already-computed answer instead of recalculating it from scratch." That fix is dynamic programming.
Dynamic programming solves a problem by breaking it into overlapping subproblems, solving each subproblem only once, and storing (remembering) its result for reuse.

rows: The same smaller subproblem is needed multiple times while solving the bigger problem — exactly Fibonacci's fibonacci(2) being recomputed repeatedly
The problem's overall solution can be built directly from the solutions to its subproblems — Fibonacci's fibonacci(n) is built directly from fibonacci(n-1) and fibonacci(n-2)

sections.text: Both properties are required. A problem with optimal substructure but NO overlapping subproblems doesn't benefit from DP — there's nothing being wastefully recomputed to save. Recall Merge Sort's divide-and-conquer from Sorting: it has optimal substructure (a sorted array is built from two sorted halves), but its subproblems don't overlap — each half is genuinely distinct — which is exactly why merge sort isn't described as "dynamic programming."
This is only a preview — Memoization and Tabulation, the two ways to actually implement DP, each get their own dedicated topic right after this.

rows: Keep the natural recursive structure, but cache each subproblem's answer the first time it's computed ("top-down")
Build the answer iteratively from the smallest subproblems upward, filling a table ("bottom-up")

sections.title: Answering the same question once, then just checking your notes
sections.text: Recall Recursion in DSA's analogy: everyone in a chain re-asking and re-answering the same question independently. Dynamic programming is that same chain, except now the first person to answer any given question writes it down — anyone asking that exact question again just reads the note instead of redoing the work from scratch.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Applying DP to a problem with no overlapping subproblems — there's nothing to save by remembering answers that are never actually needed twice.
Assuming any recursive problem automatically benefits from DP — both overlapping subproblems AND optimal substructure are required.
Confusing DP with plain recursion — plain recursion (like naive Fibonacci) can recompute the same subproblem endlessly; DP specifically adds the 'remember it' step.
Confusing DP with divide-and-conquer (like Merge Sort) — divide-and-conquer's subproblems are typically distinct, not overlapping, so there's nothing to cache.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is dynamic programming?
questions.answer: A technique that solves a problem by breaking it into overlapping subproblems, solving each one only once, and storing its result for reuse.

questions.question: What two properties must a problem have for dynamic programming to apply?
questions.answer: Overlapping subproblems (the same smaller subproblem is needed multiple times) and optimal substructure (the overall solution can be built directly from subproblem solutions).

questions.question: Why doesn't Merge Sort count as dynamic programming, despite being recursive?
questions.answer: It has optimal substructure, but its subproblems (the two halves being sorted) don't overlap — each half is genuinely distinct, so there's no repeated work to save by remembering answers.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Dynamic programming solves the exact inefficiency left open by naive Fibonacci in Recursion in DSA: it breaks a problem into overlapping subproblems and solves each one only once, remembering its result instead of recomputing it. This requires two properties: overlapping subproblems (the same smaller subproblem genuinely needed multiple times) and optimal substructure (the full solution can be built directly from subproblem solutions). Two approaches implement this — memoization, caching results within the natural recursive structure, and tabulation, building the answer iteratively from the smallest subproblems upward — both covered next.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Dynamic Programming
sections.text: DP = answer the same question once, write it down, read the note next time. Needs overlapping subproblems (repeated work to save) AND optimal substructure (answer built from smaller answers).

sections.title: Fibonacci Number
sections.problemStatement: Given n, return the n-th Fibonacci number, where F(0) = 0, F(1) = 1, and F(n) = F(n-1) + F(n-2) for n > 1.
hints: This is THE canonical example of overlapping subproblems from this lesson — naive recursion recomputes F(2) and F(3) many times over.
Optimal substructure: F(n) is built directly from F(n-1) and F(n-2) — nothing else is needed once those two are known.
Compare the naive recursive tree's exponential blowup against remembering each F(k) only once — that difference IS what this lesson's DP properties buy you.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Naive recursion — the exact inefficiency this lesson identifies, recomputing the same subproblems repeatedly.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(2^n) — exponential, from recomputing overlapping subproblems

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Bottom-up iteration, keeping only the last two values — no recomputation, no recursion stack, no array needed.

sections.title: Climbing Stairs
sections.problemStatement: You can climb 1 or 2 steps at a time. Given n steps, return the number of distinct ways to reach the top.
hints: Optimal substructure: the number of ways to reach step n is exactly ways(n-1) + ways(n-2) — the last move was either a 1-step from n-1 or a 2-step from n-2.
That recurrence is structurally identical to LC 509's Fibonacci relation — this problem IS Fibonacci, just phrased differently.
Overlapping subproblems appear the same way: computing ways(n-1) and ways(n-2) both independently need ways(n-3), and so on down the naive recursive tree.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Naive recursion mirroring the ways(n) = ways(n-1) + ways(n-2) recurrence directly.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(2^n) — exponential, identical blowup to naive Fibonacci

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Bottom-up iteration, keeping only the last two values — same technique as LC 509's optimal Fibonacci.
